title: Problem and environment characteristics

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Problem and environment characteristics; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; AI Knowledge Area: fundamental issues, intelligent agents, history, and societal context; atomic topic: Problem and environment characteristics
sources.claim: An AI task is constrained by what is observable, how actions change the environment, whether outcomes are deterministic, how time evolves, whether states/actions are discrete, how many agents act, and what uncertainty remains.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An AI task is constrained by what is observable, how actions change the environment, whether outcomes are deterministic, how time evolves, whether states/actions are discrete, how many agents act, and what uncertainty remains.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A static grid puzzle is discrete, deterministic, fully observable, and single-agent under its rules, supporting exact state-space search. Your goal is to characterize an AI task and explain how its properties constrain solution choices. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf problem io; python quality project; AI, automation and intelligence claims. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: An AI task is constrained by what is observable, how actions change the environment, whether outcomes are deterministic, how time evolves, whether states/actions are discrete, how many agents act, and what uncertainty remains. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: These properties can differ between simulation and deployment, partial observability can be hidden by convenient datasets, other agents change dynamics, and a useful abstraction deliberately omits details. The learner must reject this overclaim: Once a task is called classification, search, or robotics, its environment properties and appropriate solution family are already determined.

includes: Classify observability, determinism, dynamics, discreteness, agents and uncertainty.
Characterize an AI task and explain how its properties constrain solution choices.
excludes: These properties can differ between simulation and deployment, partial observability can be hidden by convenient datasets, other agents change dynamics, and a useful abstraction deliberately omits details.

outcomes: Characterize an AI task and explain how its properties constrain solution choices.

examples.title: Problem and environment characteristics: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A static grid puzzle is discrete, deterministic, fully observable, and single-agent under its rules, supporting exact state-space search.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A static grid puzzle is discrete, deterministic, fully observable, and single-agent under its rules, supporting exact state-space search.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Characterize observability, determinism, dynamics, discreteness, agency, and uncertainty for the actual deployment context before choosing or evaluating a method.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Characterize observability, determinism, dynamics, discreteness, agency, and uncertainty for the actual deployment context before choosing or evaluating a method.

examples.title: Problem and environment characteristics: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A road-driving task is dynamic, partially observable, continuous, stochastic, and multi-agent, so the grid assumptions cannot justify the same guarantees.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A road-driving task is dynamic, partially observable, continuous, stochastic, and multi-agent, so the grid assumptions cannot justify the same guarantees.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: These properties can differ between simulation and deployment, partial observability can be hidden by convenient datasets, other agents change dynamics, and a useful abstraction deliberately omits details.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Once a task is called classification, search, or robotics, its environment properties and appropriate solution family are already determined.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Characterize an AI task and explain how its properties constrain solution choices.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Once a task is called classification, search, or robotics, its environment properties and appropriate solution family are already determined.
misconceptions.correction: Characterize observability, determinism, dynamics, discreteness, agency, and uncertainty for the actual deployment context before choosing or evaluating a method.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Once a task is called classification, search, or robotics, its environment properties and appropriate solution family are already determined.

analogy.example: a map that labels what an automated system observes, claims, chooses, and cannot establish can introduce Problem and environment characteristics only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: separating techniques, environments, agents, evidence, and historical limits
limitations: maps simplify contested intelligence concepts and cannot turn benchmark evidence into general capability

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.

recap.summary: You should now be able to characterize an AI task and explain how its properties constrain solution choices. Keep this boundary in view: These properties can differ between simulation and deployment, partial observability can be hidden by convenient datasets, other agents change dynamics, and a useful abstraction deliberately omits details.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Problem and environment characteristics with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
Why is this overclaim invalid: Once a task is called classification, search, or robotics, its environment properties and appropriate solution family are already determined.
Apply the boundary to: A road-driving task is dynamic, partially observable, continuous, stochastic, and multi-agent, so the grid assumptions cannot justify the same guarantees.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Agent, goal and utility models”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
